name: Chandra Extended
description: Chandra, also known as Soma, is the Hindu god of the Moon. He is a lunar deity and one of the Navagraha (nine celestial beings) in Hindu astrology. Chandra is often depicted as a youthful, beautiful man, fair in complexion, holding a club and a lotus. He rides a chariot pulled by ten white horses or an antelope. He is considered the ruler of the night and is associated with dew, fertility, and emotions. Chandra is described as the son of the sage Atri and Anasuya. He is married to the twenty-seven Nakshatras (lunar mansions), who are daughters of Daksha. However, he shows preferential treatment to Rohini, causing Daksha to curse him with consumption. Shiva intervenes, mitigating the curse to waxing and waning phases. Chandra's abode is Chandraloka, a celestial realm. He is revered for his calming and nurturing qualities, and his influence is believed to affect the mind and emotions. He is also associated with the mind (Manas) itself. Chandra's ambrosia, Soma, is a sacred drink used in Vedic rituals. He is a powerful deity whose influence is felt in various aspects of life, including agriculture, medicine, and astrology. His worship is believed to bring peace, prosperity, and emotional stability. His power extends over the tides, the growth of plants, and the cycle of life. Chandra's extended lore includes tales of his involvement in various celestial battles and his interactions with other deities. He is a significant figure in Hindu cosmology and continues to be worshipped and revered by millions.
subtitle: Lunar deity of emotions and fertility

keyMyths.title: Chandra's Marriage to the Nakshatras
keyMyths.description: Chandra marries the twenty-seven daughters of Daksha but favors Rohini, leading to a curse. Shiva's intervention reduces the curse to waxing and waning phases, explaining the lunar cycle.

keyMyths.title: The Churning of the Ocean
keyMyths.description: During the churning of the ocean, Chandra emerges along with other treasures. He takes his place among the celestial bodies and becomes a significant part of the cosmos.

keyMyths.title: Chandra's Connection to Soma
keyMyths.description: Chandra is associated with Soma, the sacred drink of the gods. This connection reinforces his role as a nurturer and life-giver, linked to fertility and prosperity.

sources.author: F. Max Müller

worshipPractices.name: Chandra Puja
worshipPractices.description: Devotees perform Chandra Puja to seek blessings for peace, prosperity, and well-being. It involves chanting mantras, offering white flowers, rice, and sandalwood paste to Chandra. Fasting on Mondays dedicated to Chandra is also common.

worshipPractices.name: Soma Yajna
worshipPractices.description: The Soma Yajna is an elaborate Vedic ritual involving the extraction and offering of Soma, a sacred plant, to the deities, including Chandra (Soma). This ritual is performed to invoke divine blessings and cosmic harmony.

festivals.name: Chandra Grahan
festivals.description: Observed during lunar eclipses, devotees offer prayers to Chandra to mitigate the malefic effects associated with the eclipse. Special rituals and charitable acts are performed.

festivals.name: Sharad Purnima
festivals.timing: Full moon night in the month of Ashvin (September-October)
festivals.description: Celebrated on the full moon night, believed to be the night when Chandra showers Amrita (nectar of immortality) on Earth. People prepare kheer (rice pudding) and keep it under the moonlight, consuming it as blessed food.

cultural.modernLegacy: Chandra's influence persists in Hindu astrology, where his position in a birth chart is crucial for determining an individual's emotional nature and destiny. He is also a popular figure in modern spiritual practices, yoga, and meditation, often associated with calmness and emotional balance. The Moon continues to be revered as a symbol of beauty, serenity, and the cyclical nature of life.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: In Hindu mythology, Chandra's birth stories vary across different texts. One account states that he emerged from the churning of the Ocean of Milk (Samudra Manthan), a cosmic event where gods and demons collaborated to obtain Amrita, the nectar of immortality. Another version depicts him as the son of Sage Atri and Anasuya, renowned for her virtue and devotion. Chandra's birth is often associated with purity, radiance, and divine essence. He is sometimes linked to the lunar deity Soma, with Chandra becoming the more popular name in later Hindu traditions. These diverse origin stories highlight Chandra's significance as a fundamental element of the cosmos and a powerful symbol of life and renewal.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: Chandra is central to several prominent myths. One notable story involves his affair with Tara, the wife of Brihaspati, the guru of the gods. This illicit relationship resulted in the birth of Budha (the planet Mercury), causing a major conflict between the gods, demons, and sages. Another significant narrative recounts Chandra's curse by Daksha, who was angered by Chandra's favoritism towards his wives (the 27 Nakshatras or lunar mansions, daughters of Daksha). This curse caused Chandra to wane, symbolizing the waxing and waning phases of the moon. Lord Shiva eventually intervened to alleviate the curse, allowing Chandra to wax and wane in cycles. These myths illustrate themes of love, conflict, divine intervention, and the cyclical nature of time and existence.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: Chandra is worshipped as one of the Navagraha, the nine celestial bodies that influence human destiny according to Hindu astrology. Devotees offer prayers to Chandra to alleviate the malefic effects of his planetary position in their birth charts. White-colored items like rice, flowers, and sandalwood paste are commonly used in his worship. Mondays are considered auspicious for worshipping Chandra. Mantras dedicated to Chandra are chanted to invoke his blessings for peace, emotional stability, and overall well-being. Temples dedicated to Chandra are found throughout India, where devotees perform rituals and seek his divine grace. The worship of Chandra is deeply intertwined with astrology, agriculture, and the lunar cycles, reflecting his importance in Hindu cosmology and daily life.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Chandra's legacy extends beyond religious worship, permeating various aspects of Indian culture and thought. He is a prominent figure in classical literature, poetry, and art, often depicted as a serene and radiant deity. The lunar calendar, based on Chandra's cycles, governs many Hindu festivals and agricultural practices. In modern times, Chandra continues to inspire artists, writers, and spiritual seekers. His association with emotions, intuition, and the subconscious mind makes him a relevant symbol in contemporary psychological and spiritual discourse. The enduring reverence for Chandra underscores his profound impact on Hindu culture and his timeless appeal as a symbol of beauty, tranquility, and the cyclical nature of existence.

shortDescription: Chandra, the Hindu lunar deity, governs the night, dew, and fertility. As Soma, he embodies the Moon and is one of the Navagraha.
